FRA263 Software
  • Getting Started
  • View page source

Getting Started

From a fresh clone to a running system. This page assumes you have just cloned the repository onto a machine with nothing installed on it. It walks through the packages, the Python environment, the micro-ROS agent, and the build — then shows the two ways to run the project.

Note

This project runs on Ubuntu only. The Docker and Windows options were removed. The one place Docker still appears is the Base System front-end UI, which is shipped as a container image and has no other distribution form.

Before you start

What the machine needs. These versions go together: ROS 2 Jazzy targets Ubuntu 24.04, and both use Python 3.12. That shared Python version is what later lets one virtual environment see the ROS packages.

Item

Version

Note

Ubuntu

24.04 LTS

The distribution ROS 2 Jazzy is built for.

ROS 2

Jazzy

Installed at /opt/ros/jazzy. Older notes saying humble are out of date.

Python

3.12

Comes with Ubuntu 24.04 and is the same version ROS 2 Jazzy uses.

Docker

any recent

Only for the Base System front-end UI (Step 8). Not used anywhere else.

Step 1 — Install ROS 2 Jazzy

Follow the official Debian-package guide, which adds the apt key and the ROS 2 repository:

https://docs.ros.org/en/jazzy/Installation/Ubuntu-Install-Debs.html

Then install the desktop variant:

sudo apt update
sudo apt install ros-jazzy-desktop

Check that it is there:

source /opt/ros/jazzy/setup.bash
printenv ROS_DISTRO          # must print:  jazzy

Note

Sourcing setup.bash only affects the terminal you run it in. Every new terminal needs it again — see The environment preparation.

Step 2 — Install the system packages

# package list already refreshed in Step 1
sudo apt install -y \
    git \
    cmake \
    python3-venv \
    python3-pip \
    python3-tk \
    python3-rosdep \
    python3-colcon-common-extensions

Why these. python3-tk is the Tkinter library — mock_ui is a Tkinter window, so without it Method 1 cannot start. python3-colcon-common-extensions provides the colcon build tool. python3-rosdep resolves the micro-ROS dependencies in Step 5. cmake is used by the Teensy firmware cross-build in Step 7.

USB permission. Both the Teensy and the robot appear as USB serial devices. A normal user cannot open them until it belongs to the dialout group:

sudo usermod -aG dialout $USER

This only takes effect after you log out and back in.

Warning

Logging out closes every open application. Save your work and close your programs first. Log out the session (the same method as shutdown, but select log out instead) and back in.

Skipping this gives Permission denied: '/dev/ttyACM0' later, with nothing else to explain it.

Step 3 — Clone the repository

Clone into any folder you like, then enter it:

git clone https://github.com/Lattawat/FRA263-Demo_Software.git
cd FRA263-Demo_Software

Note

Paths on this page are relative to the project root — the FRA263-Demo_Software folder the clone just created. Open every new terminal at that root, or cd into it first, so the relative commands resolve.

What you just cloned.

FRA263-Demo_Software/
├── claude-visualizer-ws/                    Verification System — ROS 2 Jazzy workspace
│   ├── src/claude_visualizer/               nodes, launch, config, mock UI
│   ├── src/claude_visualizer_interface/     custom ROS 2 messages
│   └── encoder_data_publisher/              Teensy firmware (PlatformIO)
├── FRA263-264_BaseSystem_BackEnd/           Base System back-end (server_111.py, protocol.py)
├── FRA263-264_BaseSystem_FrontEnd/          Base System front-end (Docker image + compose)
├── docs/                                    this documentation
└── requirements.txt                         Python dependencies

See System Architecture & Overview for what each part does. build/, install/, log/ and .venv are not in the clone — you create them in the steps below.

Step 4 — Create the Python environment

The project keeps its Python packages in one virtual environment at the repository root. Run this from the project root:

python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate

Make sure that the (.venv) prompt is showing. Then install the dependencies:

pip install -r requirements.txt

requirements.txt holds every pip package the project needs — the application packages (pylsl, websockets, pymodbus, pyserial, PyYAML, numpy) and the ROS 2 build tooling (catkin_pkg, empy, lark) that colcon build needs in Step 5 and Step 6.

Note

Two sets of Python packages. The virtual environment provides the pip packages (pylsl, websockets, pymodbus, pyserial, PyYAML). ROS 2 provides rclpy and the message packages.

Sourcing /opt/ros/jazzy/setup.bash adds the ROS packages to the path Python searches, so they can be imported inside the virtual environment. This works because both use Python 3.12.

Every terminal needs both. Activate the environment only and rclpy is missing; source ROS 2 only and pylsl is missing.

Note

pylsl ships its own liblsl.so inside the pip package, so there is no separate LSL system library to install.

Step 5 — Install the micro-ROS agent

Needed only for Method 2 (with hardware). Skip it if you only want the no-hardware run.

The agent is the program that carries messages from the Teensy into the ROS 2 network.

micro-ROS is not part of this repository. It is maintained by the micro-ROS project, so you fetch and build it yourself with their official tool. The commands below add it into claude-visualizer-ws/src/ and build it next to the project packages:

Note

Keep the .venv from Step 4 active for this build. colcon build needs catkin_pkg, empy and lark, which Step 4 installed into the environment — the system does not provide them inside the environment.

cd claude-visualizer-ws
source /opt/ros/jazzy/setup.bash

# 1. Get the setup tool. The branch must match your ROS 2 distribution.
git clone -b $ROS_DISTRO https://github.com/micro-ROS/micro_ros_setup.git src/micro_ros_setup

# 2. Resolve its dependencies.
sudo rosdep init          # first time on this machine only; harmless error if already done
rosdep update
rosdep install --from-paths src --ignore-src -y

# 3. Build the setup tool.
colcon build
source install/local_setup.bash

# 4. Create and build the agent workspace. This fills src/uros/.
ros2 run micro_ros_setup create_agent_ws.sh
ros2 run micro_ros_setup build_agent.sh
source install/local_setup.bash

Check that the agent exists:

ros2 pkg list | grep micro_ros_agent      # must print:  micro_ros_agent

Step 6 — Build the workspace

This builds the Verification System packages — claude_visualizer and the custom message package claude_visualizer_interface:

cd claude-visualizer-ws
source /opt/ros/jazzy/setup.bash
colcon build
source install/setup.bash

The first build takes several minutes, mostly generating the custom message types.

Warning

Do not use --symlink-install. Always build with plain colcon build. The developer cannot remember the reason, but there are some error when install/ point back at the files in src/ instead of copying them.

If this workspace was ever built with --symlink-install, delete build/, install/ and log/ first, then build again.

Step 7 — Build and flash the Teensy firmware

Needed only for Method 2 (with hardware).

The firmware is a PlatformIO project in claude-visualizer-ws/encoder_data_publisher/. It reads the encoder and publishes /G<N>/encoder_raw. The recommended way to build and flash it is VS Code with the PlatformIO IDE extension. For how the build actually works, the command-line alternative, and how to add another message, see Teensy 4.1 Firmware.

Install the PlatformIO extension

In VS Code open Extensions, search for PlatformIO IDE, and install it. (When you open the firmware folder in the next step, VS Code also offers to install it, because the folder recommends it.)

Open the firmware in VS Code

File → Open Folder and choose claude-visualizer-ws/encoder_data_publisher. Open that folder itself, not the whole repository — PlatformIO looks for platformio.ini at the top of the folder you open. Once it loads, the blue PlatformIO toolbar appears along the bottom of the window.

Check the group number and domain before flashing

Open src/main.cpp and confirm two values — the group number near the top, and the domain ID inside setup():

#define GROUP_NUMBER 1                                  // set to your group N
rcl_init_options_set_domain_id(&init_options, 156);     // must equal ROS_DOMAIN_ID

Warning

The defaults do not match out of the box. The firmware ships with GROUP_NUMBER 1, while the launch file and the Base System back-end default to 0. Set them all to the same N, and keep the domain at 156 (the value used everywhere in this guide). If they disagree, the Teensy publishes on /G1/encoder_raw while the rest of the system listens on /G0/encoder_raw — everything starts, but no data lines up.

Allow the Teensy to be flashed (one time)

A USB device is owned by root by default, so the upload fails with a permission error until your user is allowed to access Teensy boards:

sudo curl -fsSL https://www.pjrc.com/teensy/00-teensy.rules -o /etc/udev/rules.d/00-teensy.rules
sudo udevadm control --reload-rules && sudo udevadm trigger

Build and upload

With the Teensy plugged in, use the PlatformIO toolbar at the bottom of the VS Code window:

  • ✓ (Build) compiles the firmware. The first build takes 5–15 minutes.

  • → (Upload) flashes the board.

The same actions are under the PlatformIO sidebar → Project Tasks → teensy41 → General → Build / Upload. If the Teensy Loader window opens and waits, press the button on the board to start the flash.

Step 8 — Load the front-end UI image

Needed only for Method 2 (with hardware).

The Base System front-end is distributed as a Docker image:

cd FRA263-264_BaseSystem_FrontEnd
docker load -i frontend-image_v1_2.tar      # first time only
docker compose up -d

The UI is then served at http://localhost:3000. To stop and remove the container:

docker compose down

Note

This is the only Docker in the project. The Verification System runs directly on the machine — do not use the compose files under claude-visualizer-ws/docker/, which belong to the removed container option.

Step 9 — Check the installation

These checks need no hardware. Run them from the project root; each line tells you which step failed. The real functional test is Method 1 below — if it draws charts, the install is good.

# from the project root
source /opt/ros/jazzy/setup.bash
source claude-visualizer-ws/install/setup.bash
source .venv/bin/activate

printenv ROS_DISTRO                                   # Step 1 -> jazzy
ros2 pkg list | grep -E "claude|micro_ros_agent"      # Steps 5, 6
python3 -c "import pylsl, websockets, pymodbus, serial, yaml, numpy; print('python deps OK')"

ros2 pkg list should show claude_visualizer and claude_visualizer_interface, plus micro_ros_agent if you did Step 5.

Running the system

The environment preparation

Every new terminal starts the same way. A terminal that skips a line here will fail with a missing command or a missing module, so run all four in each terminal before the commands in the run methods below:

# from the project root
source /opt/ros/jazzy/setup.bash                       # ROS 2
source claude-visualizer-ws/install/setup.bash         # this workspace
source .venv/bin/activate                              # Python packages
export ROS_DOMAIN_ID=156                               # must match the Teensy

ROS_DOMAIN_ID is not decoration. The Teensy firmware joins domain 156, and nodes on different domains cannot see each other, so a wrong value here produces a system that starts cleanly and shows nothing.

In the commands below, N is your group number. Use 0 if you have not been given one.

Method 1 — Run without hardware

Use this for debugging and development. mock_ui stands in for both the Teensy and the robot: it publishes encoder_raw and it sends the two LSL streams. There is no micro-ROS agent, no Base System back-end, and no front-end UI in this mode.

Terminal 1 - mock_ui.py                Terminal 2 - ros2 launch bringup
  (Tkinter window)                       encoder_reader
     |  /G<N>/encoder_raw  ------------>      |  /G<N>/estimated_states
     |                                        +--> experiment_evaluator
     |  LSL ActualStates_N               web_visualizer
     |      EventTrigger_N  ----------->      |
                                              v
                                     Verifier UI  http://localhost:8000

Terminal 1 — the mock UI. Note it is started with python3; it is not installed as a ROS executable:

cd claude-visualizer-ws
python3 src/claude_visualizer/mock_UI/mock_ui.py --group-number N

Terminal 2 — the ROS 2 pipeline:

cd claude-visualizer-ws
ros2 launch claude_visualizer bringup.launch.py group_number:=N

Open http://localhost:8000. Drag the knobs in the mock UI window and the charts move.

The knobs, the sync toggle, the reset buttons and the command field are described on the mock_ui_script.rst page.

Method 2 — Run with hardware

This is the full system. Plug in the Teensy and the robot, identify their serial ports (below), then start the four terminals in order. Each one uses the environment preparation above.

Identify the serial ports

Both the Teensy and the robot appear as /dev/ttyACM* devices, and the numbering depends on the order they were plugged in — so you have to find out which is which. The simplest way is to plug them in one at a time:

ls /dev/ttyACM*        # nothing plugged in: "No such file or directory"
# plug in the Teensy, then:
ls /dev/ttyACM*        # the new entry is the Teensy   (e.g. /dev/ttyACM0)
# plug in the robot, then:
ls /dev/ttyACM*        # the second entry is the robot (e.g. /dev/ttyACM1)

If both are already connected, udevadm tells them apart — the Teensy reports a Teensyduino / PJRC model, the robot an STMicroelectronics one:

udevadm info -q property -n /dev/ttyACM0 | grep -E "ID_VENDOR|ID_MODEL"

The two ports are used in two different places:

  • The micro-ROS agent (Terminal 1) reads the Teensy — give it that port with --dev.

  • The Base System back-end takes no port on the command line. You connect the robot from the browser UI (Terminal 4): in the connect dialog, enter the robot’s ttyACM number and the slave address 21. The back-end builds /dev/ttyACM{number} from it — so a robot at /dev/ttyACM1 means you enter port 1.

Terminal 1 - micro-ROS agent
  Teensy 4.1  --USB serial 115200-->  micro_ros_agent  -->  /G<N>/encoder_raw

Terminal 2 - ROS 2 pipeline  (ros2 launch claude_visualizer bringup.launch.py)
  /G<N>/encoder_raw --> encoder_reader --> /G<N>/estimated_states
                                               |
                                               +--> experiment_evaluator --> /G<N>/eval_*
                                               +--> web_visualizer --> Verifier UI :8000

Terminal 3 - Base System back-end  (python3 server_111.py --group_number N)
  Robot (STM32) <--Modbus RTU 230400--> server_111.py
                                           +--WebSocket :8765--> Front-end UI
                                           +--LSL ActualStates_N / EventTrigger_N
                                                                 --> web_visualizer

Terminal 4 - Front-end UI  (docker compose up -d)
  Browser :3000 <-- React UI container

Terminal 1 — micro-ROS agent (Teensy bridge). Use your Teensy port (/dev/ttyACM0 here):

cd claude-visualizer-ws
ros2 run micro_ros_agent micro_ros_agent serial --dev /dev/ttyACM0 -b 115200

Terminal 2 — ROS 2 pipeline:

cd claude-visualizer-ws
ros2 launch claude_visualizer bringup.launch.py group_number:=N

Terminal 3 — Base System back-end:

cd FRA263-264_BaseSystem_BackEnd
python3 server_111.py --group_number N

Terminal 4 — Base System front-end:

cd FRA263-264_BaseSystem_FrontEnd
docker compose up -d

Then open both pages: the verifier dashboard at http://localhost:8000 and the Base System UI at http://localhost:3000. In the Base System UI, connect to the robot using its port number and slave 21 — see “Identify the serial ports” above.

Ports and addresses

Link

Address

Used by

Verifier dashboard (HTTP)

http://localhost:8000

web_visualizer serves the page.

Verifier live data (WebSocket)

ws://localhost:9090

The dashboard finds this itself from /config.json.

Base System UI (HTTP)

http://localhost:3000

The front-end container.

Base System back-end (WebSocket)

ws://localhost:8765

Between the front-end UI and server_111.py.

Teensy (USB serial)

/dev/ttyACM*, 115200

Between the Teensy and micro_ros_agent.

Robot (Modbus RTU)

/dev/ttyACM*, 230400 8-E-1, slave 21

Between server_111.py and the STM32.

The web ports are fixed. Different machines have different IP addresses, so groups do not need different ports.

Keeping one group consistent

One number has to agree in four places. The group number sets the ROS namespace /G<N>/, the LSL stream suffix _N, and the row read from criteria.yaml. If two of these disagree, the parts start normally but never find each other.

Where

How it is set

Method

ROS 2 pipeline

ros2 launch ... group_number:=N

1 and 2

Mock UI

python3 mock_ui.py --group-number N

1

Base System back-end

python3 server_111.py --group_number N

2

Teensy firmware

GROUP_NUMBER in the firmware source

2

Warning

The three tools spell the option differently. The launch file takes group_number:=N (a ROS launch argument), the Base System back-end takes --group_number N with an underscore, and the mock UI takes --group-number N with a hyphen. Using the wrong one is not silently ignored — the mock UI exits with unrecognized arguments. Copy the exact line from the run method above.

N = 0 is the default: namespace /G0/, no LSL suffix, and the default criteria row. On top of this, ROS_DOMAIN_ID must be 156 on every machine, matching the firmware.

Troubleshooting

Symptom

Cause

Fix

ros2: command not found

ROS 2 not sourced in this terminal.

source /opt/ros/jazzy/setup.bash

ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'pylsl'

The virtual environment is not active.

source .venv/bin/activate (from the project root)

ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'rclpy'

ROS 2 not sourced (the environment alone does not provide it).

Source ROS 2 as well — see The environment preparation.

colcon build: No module named 'catkin_pkg' / 'em' / 'lark'

The build tooling is not in the active virtual environment.

Activate .venv and re-run pip install -r requirements.txt (Step 4).

Package 'micro_ros_agent' not found

Step 5 was skipped — micro-ROS is not part of the clone.

Run Step 5.

Permission denied: '/dev/ttyACM0'

The user is not in the dialout group.

sudo usermod -aG dialout $USER, then log out and back in.

Everything starts, but the charts stay empty

Group number or ROS_DOMAIN_ID does not match across the parts.

Check all four places above, and ROS_DOMAIN_ID=156 everywhere.

A node runs old code, or a renamed file is still loaded

The workspace was built with --symlink-install.

Delete build/, install/, log/ and run colcon build again.

Port 3000 or 8765 already in use

Another copy of the UI or back-end is still running.

docker compose down; stop the other server_111.py.

colcon: command not found

The colcon package is missing.

sudo apt install python3-colcon-common-extensions

Where to go next

  • Verification System Implementation — the design of each node in Verification System.

  • Teensy 4.1 Firmware — the Teensy 4.1 firmware in detail.
